glossary.definition: a famous example of criticism. Searle describes a situation in which the physical system correctly manipulates physical symbols, but the system has no intelligence. The idea of the argument is the following. Imagine a room in which there is a person who receives an input through one slot and produces an output through another slot. Both input and output are represented as a piece of paper with Chinese symbols written on it. The person does not know Chinese and cannot understand what is written. He/she uses an instruction manual written in English that tells the person in the room how to respond to the paper received. Therefore, the only thing the person does is to match the Chinese symbols received with symbols presented in the instruction manual in some sort of formal way – according to their shape, for example. The matching operation allows him/her to identify which symbols to produce as an output. Therefore, the person does not understand the written symbols but is able to provide the right output. To someone outside the room it might look as if there is intelligent behaviour going on. In reality, there is no intelligence.
glossary.sentence: Critics claim that the hypothesis is not enough to simulate human intelligence because intelligence is genetically biological and cannot be fixed by symbols (Luger, 2009) . John Searle’s Chinese room argument is a famous example of criticism.

glossary.definition: simplified models of the human brain consisting of a large number of connected processing units (artificial neurons) that mimic the neurons of the human brain, and numerical weights that are attached to the connections between the units, reflecting the strength of these connections
glossary.sentence: This approach has emerged from attempts to mathematically model neural activity in the human brain and is based on so-called artificial neural networks. Artificial neural networks are simplified models of the human brain consisting of a large number of connected processing units (artificial neurons) that mimic the neurons of the human brain, and numerical weights that are attached to the connections between the units, reflecting the strength of these connections.

glossary.definition: In the case of a partial state space graph, heuristic measures of nodes are obtained by using a heuristic evaluation function. A heuristic measure assigned to the node representing the current state of the game indicates only the best state that can be reached after making n moves from the current state. It is not a game result!
glossary.sentence: In the case of a partial state space graph, heuristic measures of nodes are obtained by using a heuristic evaluation function. The result of assigning heuristic measures to nodes In the case of a complete state space graph, a heuristic measure assigned to the root node is called a game result . It clearly shows the result of the game if both players play rationally, without making mistakes, and with the aim of winning the game. In the case of a partial state space graph, a heuristic measure assigned to the node representing the current state of the game indicates only the best state that can be reached after making n moves from the current state. It is not a game result! Identifying paths In the case of the complete state space graph, winning paths are identified based on the game result. These are not paths that definitely lead to a win by a particular player; they are paths showing the course of the game that is most likely to be undertaken. A winning path is a path that starts at the root node and reaches a leaf node while satisfying the following conditions: all nodes in the winning path are closely related; all nodes in the winning path have the same heuristic measures, which are equal to the game result. In the case of a partial state space graph, when the best state has been identified, paths leading to this state can be determined. These are not winning paths! These are paths leading to the best state that can be obtained after making n moves from the current state.

glossary.definition: In the case of the complete state space graph, winning paths are identified based on the game result. These are not paths that definitely lead to a win by a particular player; they are paths showing the course of the game that is most likely to be undertaken. A winning path is a path that starts at the root node and reaches a leaf node while satisfying the following conditions: all nodes in the winning path are closely related; all nodes in the winning path have the same heuristic measures, which are equal to the game result.
glossary.sentence: In the case of the complete state space graph, winning paths are identified based on the game result. A winning path is a path that starts at the root node and reaches a leaf node while satisfying the following conditions: all nodes in the winning path are closely related; all nodes in the winning path have the same heuristic measures, which are equal to the game result.

glossary.definition: The minimax algorithm is one of the simplest of the search-based game algorithms. It involves the following steps (Luger, 2009): create a complete state space graph; label the levels of the graph as MIN and MAX; assign heuristic measures to all leaf nodes according to an analysis of who wins at a specific node; propagate heuristic measures of leaf nodes up to the root node of the state space graph according to the following rules: a) if a node is located at MAX level, it acquires the maximum heuristic measure from among its direct descendants, and b) if a node is located at MIN level, it acquires the minimum heuristic measure from among its direct descendants; determine the result of the game and the winning paths.
glossary.sentence: The minimax algorithm is one of the simplest of the search-based game algorithms. It involves the following steps (Luger, 2009): create a complete state space graph; label the levels of the graph as MIN and MAX; assign heuristic measures to all leaf nodes according to an analysis of who wins at a specific node; propagate heuristic measures of leaf nodes up to the root node of the state space graph according to the following rules: a) if a node is located at MAX level, it acquires the maximum heuristic measure from among its direct descendants, and b) if a node is located at MIN level, it acquires the minimum heuristic measure from among its direct descendants; determine the result of the game and the winning paths.

glossary.definition: Alpha-beta pruning is an alternative to the minimax algorithm that improves the search efficiency of the state space graph of the game. It is based on the idea that moves that are not beneficial (or in other words, moves that make no difference to the final decision) can be ignored (Jones, 2009; Russell and Norvig, 2010). Alpha-beta pruning therefore returns the same move as minimax, but cuts off unpromising paths from the state space graph (Luger, 2009). As a result, it does not give heuristic measures for all nodes. This algorithm uses the same rules for propagating heuristic measures (Luger, 2009): if a node is located at MAX level, it acquires the maximum heuristic measure from among its direct descendants; if a node is located at MIN level, it acquires the minimum heuristic measure from among its direct descendants.
glossary.sentence: Alpha-beta pruning is an alternative to the minimax algorithm that improves the search efficiency of the state space graph of the game. It is based on the idea that moves that are not beneficial (or in other words, moves that make no difference to the final decision) can be ignored (Jones, 2009; Russell and Norvig, 2010). Alpha-beta pruning therefore returns the same move as minimax, but cuts off unpromising paths from the state space graph (Luger, 2009). As a result, it does not give heuristic measures for all nodes. This algorithm uses the same rules for propagating heuristic measures (Luger, 2009): if a node is located at MAX level, it acquires the maximum heuristic measure from among its direct descendants; if a node is located at MIN level, it acquires the minimum heuristic measure from among its direct descendants.

glossary.definition: A central point of a cluster, defined as the average of the feature vectors of all data objects within the cluster.

glossary.definition: A method used to quantify the similarity or dissimilarity between two data objects, such as Euclidean, Manhattan, or Hamming distance.

glossary.definition: the final grouping of data objects determined by the number of vertical lines intersected by a horizontal cut-off line in a dendrogram

glossary.definition: The nature of data attributes, either categorical or continuous, which determines the appropriate distance metric used in similarity calculations.

glossary.definition: a clustering method that starts with each data object in its own cluster and recursively merges pairs of clusters with the smallest intergroup dissimilarity until one cluster containing all data objects is formed
glossary.sentence: Therefore, each data object initially is attributed to its own cluster. Then, at each level, a selected pair of clusters is recursively merged into a single cluster. This produces a grouping at the next higher level with one less cluster.

glossary.definition: a clustering method that starts with a cluster containing all data objects and recursively splits one existing cluster into two new clusters at each hierarchy level, choosing the split that produces two groups with the largest between-group dissimilarity
glossary.sentence: Divisive hierarchical clustering applies a top-down clustering approach. Therefore, it starts with a cluster with all data objects inside it. Then, at each hierarchy level, it recursively splits one of the existing clusters into two new clusters.

glossary.definition: the measure of dissimilarity between two clusters, used to select pairs of clusters for merging in agglomerative hierarchical clustering
glossary.sentence: a pair of clusters is merged at each hierarchy level until one cluster with all data objects is acquired at the highest level. The pairs chosen for merging consist of the two groups with the smallest intergroup dissimilarity

glossary.definition: the measure of dissimilarity between two groups of data objects, used to determine the split in divisive hierarchical clustering that produces the largest between-group dissimilarity
glossary.sentence: The split is chosen to produce two new groups with the largest between-group dissimilarity

glossary.definition: methods used to measure the similarity (distance) between clusters to decide the rules for merging clusters in hierarchical clustering

glossary.definition: a linkage method that calculates the distance between clusters as the distance between the most distant elements of the two clusters (the longest distance)
glossary.sentence: Complete-linkage: in deciding about the cluster similarity, the distance between the clusters’ most distant elements (the longest distance) is calculated

glossary.definition: a linkage method that calculates the distance between clusters as the distance between the closest elements of the two clusters (the shortest distance)
glossary.sentence: Single-linkage: in deciding about the cluster similarity, the distance between the closest elements of the two clusters (the shortest distance) is calculated

glossary.definition: a linkage method that defines the distance between two clusters as the average distance between each data object in one cluster to every data object in the other cluster
glossary.sentence: Average-linkage: the distance between two clusters is defined as the average distance between each data object in one cluster to every data object in the other cluster

glossary.definition: an output of hierarchical clustering that is a tree-like diagram representing hierarchical relationships between data objects in the dataset
glossary.sentence: A dendrogram is an output of hierarchical clustering. It is a tree-like diagram representing hierarchical relationships between data objects in the dataset.

glossary.definition: a branch in a dendrogram that represents the merging of data objects into a cluster, with each clade having a height indicating the level of similarity

glossary.definition: a terminal point in a dendrogram corresponding to a data object used in the clustering process

glossary.definition: the vertical position of a clade in a dendrogram, which serves as an indicator of cluster similarity; the smaller the height, the more similar the data objects in that clade
glossary.sentence: The key to interpreting a dendrogram is to focus on the height of clades, that is, the height at which two data objects are merged. The height of clades serves as an indicator of cluster similarity: Two data objects (leaves) in the same clade are more similar than two data objects (leaves) in another clade.

glossary.definition: a line drawn through a dendrogram to decide the number of clusters based on the number of vertical lines it intersects
glossary.sentence: A horizontal cut- off line is usually made through the dendrogram to decide the number of clusters based on the hierarchical clustering algorithm.

glossary.definition: a specific level in the hierarchy of a dendrogram at which a horizontal cut-off line is placed to determine the number of clusters
glossary.sentence: Cut-offs can be performed at different levels of the hierarchy leading to a different number of clusters. For example, in Figure 6, Cut-off 1 intersects two vertical lines, and we receive two clusters with the following data objects: (A, B, C, D) and (E).

glossary.definition: metrics used by developers to assess the performance of a clustering algorithm by evaluating different numbers of clusters
glossary.sentence: to make the right cut-off, the developers usually need to use evaluation metrics evaluating the algorithm's performance with the different number of clusters

glossary.definition: an ordered sequence of cluster merges that represents the entire process of hierarchical clustering
glossary.sentence: the entire cluster hierarchy represents an ordered sequence of cluster merges

glossary.definition: a method used recursively to implement divisive hierarchical clustering by performing the procedure on each intermediate cluster until all data objects are in clusters or the minimum number of data objects per cluster is reached
glossary.sentence: one of the methods to implement this type of hierarchical clustering is to perform the procedure of K-Means recursively on each intermediate cluster till you encounter all the data objects in the dataset or the minimum number of data objects you desire to have in a cluster

glossary.definition: a stage in the hierarchical clustering process at which clusters are either merged or split
glossary.sentence: at each level, a selected pair of clusters is recursively merged into a single cluster

glossary.term: data objects with the largest distance
glossary.definition: data objects within a cluster that are farthest from other data objects, which are selected for splitting in divisive hierarchical clustering
glossary.sentence: data objects having the largest distance from other data objects in a cluster are split in a separate cluster at each step of clustering

glossary.definition: The typical approach in multiclass classification task is to consider each class as a binary case based on One-vs-Rest approaches in which the class under consideration is treated to be a positive one while other negative ones.
glossary.sentence: Therefore, further in the text, the metrics will be generally considered for the binary case, if necessary, also providing a reference to the multiclass classification task.

glossary.definition: True positives (TP) – a data object is positive (label 1), and the algorithm correctly recognises it as such (label 1). For example, an e-mail is classified as spam, and it is spam.
glossary.sentence: Correct predictions: True positives (TP) – a data object is positive (label 1), and the algorithm correctly recognises it as such (label 1). For example, an e-mail is classified as spam, and it is spam.

glossary.definition: True negatives (TN) - a data object is negative (label 0), and the algorithm correctly recognises it as such (label 0). For example, an e-mail is classified as non-spam, and it is non-spam.
glossary.sentence: True negatives (TN) - a data object is negative (label 0), and the algorithm correctly recognises it as such (label 0). For example, an e-mail is classified as non-spam, and it is non-spam.

glossary.definition: False positives (FP) – a data object is negative (label 0), but the algorithm labels it as positive (label 1); for example, an e-mail is classified as spam, but it is not. In this case, we can say that a data object is "falsely" predicted as positive.
glossary.sentence: False positives (FP) – a data object is negative (label 0), but the algorithm labels it as positive (label 1); for example, an e-mail is classified as spam, but it is not. In this case, we can say that a data object is "falsely" predicted as positive.

glossary.definition: False negatives (FN) – a data object is positive (label 1), but the classifier labels it as negative (label 0), for example, an e-mail is classified as non-spam, but it is spam. In this case, we can say that a data object is "falsely" predicted as negative.
glossary.sentence: False negatives (FN) – a data object is positive (label 1), but the classifier labels it as negative (label 0), for example, an e-mail is classified as non-spam, but it is spam. In this case, we can say that a data object is "falsely" predicted as negative.

glossary.definition: The mentioned outcomes are typically summarised in a confusion matrix (Kubat, 2017). It is a two-dimensional table of counts of how often each class/category is classified or misclassified as each other category (Russell & Norvig. 2010). Thus, it represents detailed information on the number of correct and erroneous predictions for each class/category. Each row in a confusion matrix represents an actual class, while each column represents a predicted class (Geron, 2017). The structure of the matrix differs a little bit for the binary classification task and multiclass classification. Table 1 represents the structure of the confusion matrix for the binary classification, while Table 2 gives an example of the matrix. In multiclass classification, the main diagonal of the confusion matrix represents the number of data objects correctly predicted (true positives for a specific class/category). Still, other cells correspond to the number of data objects mislabelled by the algorithm. Taking a specific class/category, we can consider the values at the intersections of the column of the corresponding class with other classes as false positives, while values at the intersections of the row of the corresponding class with other classes as false negatives. Other cells that do not intersect the column and row corresponding to a specific class/category represent true negatives for this specific class. Table 3 represents these ideas regarding Class B. Table 4 gives an example of the confusion matrix for the multiclass classification task. Thus, the higher the diagonal values of the confusion matrix regarding the total number of data objects in the dataset, the better the algorithm performance is. A perfect classifier would have only true positives and true negatives in a binary classification task (Geron, 2017).
glossary.sentence: The mentioned outcomes are typically summarised in a confusion matrix (Kubat, 2017).

glossary.definition: Accuracy metric evaluates a fraction of correct predictions over a given set of data objects. It is calculated in the following way (Kubat, 2017): Accuracy for the multiclass classification is calculated by summing up values of the main diagonal of the confusion matrix and dividing it by the total number of data objects in the dataset. The value of accuracy lies in the range [0,1]. A larger value indicates better predictive accuracy. However, this metrics is not a clear indicator of the performance, especially if classes are imbalanced (Kubat, 2017).
glossary.sentence: Accuracy metric evaluates a fraction of correct predictions over a given set of data objects.

glossary.definition: A function that combines input signals received by a neuron to produce a single value that is then used as input for the activation function.

glossary.sentence: The transfer (activation) function determines the output of the neuron.

glossary.sentence: the perceptron processes it based on the summation and activation functions available in the processing unit

glossary.definition: An activation function that outputs a discrete value, often used in decision-making neurons for classification and pattern recognition tasks.
glossary.sentence: The step and sign activation functions, also called hard limit functions, are often used in decision-making neurons for classification and pattern recognition tasks.

glossary.definition: An activation function that outputs a value of either positive or negative based on the input, also known as hard limit functions.
glossary.sentence: The step and sign activation functions, also called hard limit functions, are often used in decision-making neurons for classification and pattern recognition tasks.

glossary.sentence: The four common choices are the step, sign, linear and sigmoid functions.

glossary.definition: A system comprising one or more artificial neurons connected in a pattern, capable of performing complex tasks through the interaction of many simple processing units.
glossary.sentence: An artificial neural network comprises one or more artificial neurons connected in some pattern.

glossary.definition: A single processing unit in an artificial neural network that receives inputs, applies a summation and activation function, and produces an output.
glossary.sentence: Single neurons are called processing units or nodes. Every neuron applies the summation and activation functions and receives an output that is sent to other neurons again.

glossary.definition: a neuron that performs computation by calculating the weighted sum including the bias input and then applying the activation function
glossary.sentence: each processing unit calculates the weighted sum, including the bias input, and then applies the activation function

glossary.definition: A type of artificial neural network with connections only in one direction, where each processing unit receives input from upstream units and delivers output to downstream units, with no loops.
glossary.sentence: A feed-forward network has connections only in one direction. Every processing unit receives input from 'upstream' processing units and delivers output to 'downstream' processing units; there are no loops.

glossary.definition: A type of artificial neural network that feeds its outputs back into its inputs, forming a dynamic system that may reach a stable state or exhibit oscillations or chaotic behaviour.

glossary.definition: A method applied to modify the weights of the network when there is a difference between the desired output and the actual output of the network.
glossary.sentence: In case if there is a difference between the desired output and the output of the network, a learning rule is applied to modify the weights of the network.

glossary.definition: The structure of an artificial neural network defined by the number of layers, the number of processing units, and the connectivity pattern.
glossary.sentence: The main steps of creating and using artificial neural networks are: to select the architecture of the network: the number of layers, the number of processing units and the connectivity pattern;

glossary.definition: The pattern in which processing units are connected to each other in an artificial neural network.
glossary.sentence: The main steps of creating and using artificial neural networks are: to select the architecture of the network: the number of layers, the number of processing units and the connectivity pattern;

glossary.definition: A task in which an input object is assigned to a category based on its feature values.

glossary.definition: A task that involves identifying the structure or pattern in data.
glossary.sentence: Artificial neural networks can be applied to a diversity of tasks: pattern recognition which assumes identifying the structure or pattern in data;

glossary.sentence: Artificial neural networks can be applied to a diversity of tasks: noise filtering, or separating signal from background, factoring out the irrelevant components of a signal;

glossary.definition: A task of determining values of variables that cannot be measured directly but are known to depend in a complex way on other accessible variables.
glossary.sentence: Artificial neural networks can be applied to a diversity of tasks: non-linear estimation determining the values of variables that cannot be measured easily but which are known to depend in some complex way on other more accessible variables and others.

glossary.definition: The difference between the right output and the predicted output of a network, indicating a mismatch between expected and actual results.

glossary.definition: A machine learning task in which the dataset contains data objects together with the right output labels, allowing the system to learn by comparing predicted outputs with actual outputs.

glossary.definition: The process of propagating error from the output layer backward through the network layers to adjust the weights in all layers to reduce the total error.
glossary.sentence: the backpropagation algorithm allows modifying weights in all network layers through the propagation of the error from the output layer to the input layer

glossary.definition: A neuron in the output layer of a neural network that produces a predicted output value based on the inputs and weights of the network.
glossary.sentence: In the output neuron, we know the predicted output and the real class label and, thus, can calculate the error

glossary.definition: The iterative process of adjusting network weights to minimize error by repeatedly feeding training examples and updating weights based on the error calculated during backpropagation.
glossary.sentence: the training process continues until some termination criterion is achieved

glossary.definition: The way in which knowledge is described using mathematical language to allow intelligent systems to use and process it effectively.
glossary.sentence: knowledge should be described in a formal (mathematical) way with the aim of allowing the intelligent system to use and process it

glossary.definition: An intelligent computer system that contains a knowledge base and an inference engine to support problem-solving, answering questions, or building action plans using stored knowledge.
glossary.sentence: A knowledge-based intelligent system is an intelligent computer system that contains two main components – the knowledge base and the inference engine

glossary.definition: Entities and relationships that exist in the physical world and are mapped to computational objects and relations within a knowledge base for processing by an intelligent system.
glossary.sentence: The knowledge base maps objects and relations that exist in the real world to computational objects and relations.

glossary.definition: A human memory system with unlimited capacity that stores knowledge over long periods and is used as a model for knowledge storage in intelligent systems.
glossary.sentence: Humans gain knowledge in different ways and keep it in the long-term memory.

glossary.definition: The operational capabilities of an intelligent system, such as explaining problem solutions or maintaining communication with users, which necessitate the representation and use of knowledge.
glossary.sentence: the necessity to represent and use knowledge is dictated by the functionality of intelligent computer systems

glossary.term: early computer programs in artificial intelligence
glossary.definition: Initial programs in the field of artificial intelligence that relied on simple syntactical manipulation and basic reasoning steps, which eventually led to a crisis in the field when they proved insufficient.
glossary.sentence: Early computer programs in the field of artificial intelligence were based on simple syntactical manipulation and mechanisms for joining basic reasoning steps, which initially produced good results, but ultimately were exhausted and led to a crisis in the field of artificial intelligence when research works were stopped.

glossary.definition: The difference between the predicted output and the real class label in an output neuron, calculated using the known predicted output and real class label.
glossary.sentence: In the output neuron, we know the predicted output and the real class label and, thus, can calculate the error

glossary.definition: The error contribution of a hidden processing unit to the total error of the output units, calculated based on the error propagation from the output layer.
glossary.sentence: each hidden processing unit is responsible for some fraction of the error in each of the output units

glossary.definition: A system that stores unrelated facts, for example, purchase transactions or grades of students.
glossary.sentence: The knowledge base differs from a database. The database stores unrelated facts, for example, purchase transactions or grades of students.

glossary.definition: A connection or association between objects in a problem domain, such as Father-Son and Husband-Wife in the example of human relationships.
glossary.sentence: In the problem domain of human relationships (Figure 2), there are three objects - John, Hugo and Mary, and two relationships between them - Father-Son and Husband-Wife.

glossary.definition: A method or structure used to represent knowledge in a system
glossary.sentence: A semantic network is a knowledge representation schema that represents knowledge as a graph

glossary.definition: The process of adding knowledge into an intelligent computer system, which belongs to the so-called strong methods of artificial intelligence.
glossary.sentence: Incorporating knowledge into an intelligent computer system belongs to the so-called strong methods of artificial intelligence.

glossary.definition: Intelligent computer systems that learn from examples and do not require a knowledge base, representing examples of systems developed using weak methods.
glossary.sentence: Machine learning applications and search-based planning systems are examples of such systems.

glossary.definition: Intelligent computer systems that use search methods to plan sequences of actions to achieve goals, representing examples of systems developed using weak methods.
glossary.sentence: Machine learning applications and search-based planning systems are examples of such systems.

glossary.definition: The process by which knowledge is obtained and added to a knowledge base through several main methods: acquisition from a domain expert, use of an automated knowledge acquisition system, or learning from examples.
glossary.sentence: How does knowledge get to the knowledge base? There are several main methods used for this purpose (Hopgood, 2001): 1) knowledge is acquired from a domain expert, processed by a specialist called a knowledge engineer and then added to the knowledge base, 2) the domain expert can describe and store knowledge using an automated knowledge acquisition system, and 3) nowadays intelligent computer systems are able to learn from examples.

glossary.definition: A person who has deep knowledge (of both facts and rules) and substantial practical experience in a particular (usually limited) domain, and who may possess domain-specific heuristic knowledge allowing effective problem solving.
glossary.sentence: Anyone can be considered a domain expert if he/she has deep knowledge (of both facts and rules) and substantial practical experience in a particular (usually limited) domain (Negnevitsky, 2011). Moreover, the domain expert has domain-specific heuristic knowledge that allows him to solve problems in a more effective way.

glossary.definition: A specialist who knows well methods and tools of artificial intelligence, as well as who has knowledge how to describe and represent knowledge in the knowledge base, and who works in close collaboration with the domain expert.
glossary.sentence: The knowledge engineer is a specialist who knows well methods and tools of artificial intelligence, as well as who has knowledge how to describe and represent knowledge in the knowledge base. However, the knowledge engineer is not a specialist in all problem domains. This is a reason why he/she works in a close collaboration with the domain expert.

glossary.definition: An automated system that can be used either by the domain expert directly or by the knowledge engineer to describe knowledge and build a knowledge base, offering user interfaces such as diagram creation and structured knowledge descriptions, and including tools for knowledge analysis, generation, and consistency checking.
glossary.sentence: The domain expert can describe and store knowledge using an automated knowledge acquisition system, and such systems can offer a variety of user interfaces, for example, they could offer creation of diagrams representing some scenarios from the problem domain or the creation of structured description of knowledge. Moreover, such systems include a set of tools used for the analysis and generation of knowledge and checking the consistency of a created representation.

glossary.definition: An intelligent computer system that emulates the human expert’s methodology and performance and solves problems in a narrowly specialised domain in a way and at a performance level similar to one of the human experts.
glossary.sentence: Intelligent computer systems that emulate the human expert’s methodology and performance are called expert systems (Luger, 2009). An expert system is a knowledge-based intelligent system that solves problems in a narrowly specialised domain in a way and at a performance level similar to one of the human experts (Luger, 2009; Negnevitsky, 2011).

glossary.definition: The process of forming high-level conclusions from collections of raw data.
glossary.sentence: Expert systems address a variety of problems ((Waterman, 1986) as cited in (Luger, 2009)): Interpretation - forming high-level conclusions from collections of raw data.

glossary.definition: The process of determining the cause of malfunctions in complex situations based on observable symptoms.
glossary.sentence: Diagnosis - determining the cause of malfunctions in complex situations based on observable symptoms.

glossary.definition: The process of finding a configuration of system components that meets performance goals while satisfying a set of design constraints.
glossary.sentence: Design - finding a configuration of system components that meets performance goals while satisfying a set of design constraints.

glossary.definition: The process of devising a sequence of actions that will achieve a set of goals given certain starting conditions and run-time constraints.
glossary.sentence: Planning - devising a sequence of actions that will achieve a set of goals given certain starting conditions and run-time constraints.

glossary.definition: The process of comparing a system’s observed behaviour to its expected behaviour, for example, in instruction-assisting in the education process in technical domains.
glossary.sentence: Monitoring - comparing a system’s observed behaviour to its expected behaviour (for example, instruction-assisting in the education process in technical domains).

glossary.definition: The process of governing the behaviour of a complex environment.
glossary.sentence: Control - governing the behaviour of a complex environment.

glossary.definition: A schema that represents knowledge in the form of logical statements using constants, variables, functions, predicates, logical operators, and applies inference rules to derive new true knowledge from a set of true knowledge.
glossary.sentence: Logical knowledge representation schemas represent knowledge in the form of logical statements, using constants, variables, functions, predicates, logical operators, and apply inference rules that allow getting new true knowledge from a set of true knowledge.

glossary.definition: A schema that represents knowledge in the form of a directed labelled graph, where each node displays a simple object/concept and directed labelled arcs represent relations between them, based on associationist theories of human mind.
glossary.sentence: Networked knowledge representation schemas represent knowledge in the form of a directed labelled graph, where each node displays a simple object/concept and directed labelled arcs represent relations between them; networked knowledge representation schemas are based on associationist theories of human mind that were developed in psychology.

glossary.definition: A method that uses a graph to represent knowledge, where each node represents a complex structure describing an object/concept together with its properties and parts
glossary.sentence: Structured knowledge representation schemas also use a graph to represent knowledge. However, comparing to the networked knowledge representation schemas, each node in the graph represents not a single concept/object, but a complex structure describing an object/concept together with its properties and parts

glossary.term: procedural or rule-based knowledge representation schema
glossary.definition: A schema that represents knowledge in the form of a set of procedures/instructions.
glossary.sentence: Procedural or rule-based knowledge representation schemas represent knowledge in the form of a set of procedures/instructions.

glossary.definition: Knowledge stored at the level of a concept it belongs to
glossary.sentence: The researchers also have revealed that exceptional knowledge is stored at the level of a concept it belongs to.

glossary.definition: The time it takes to respond to a question
glossary.sentence: The mean reaction time to the question "Can an ostrich fly?" was smaller than to the question "Can an ostrich breathe?"

glossary.definition: A knowledge representation schema that represents knowledge as a graph: labelled nodes in this graph correspond to domain objects/concepts but directed labelled arcs – to relations between them
glossary.sentence: A semantic network is a knowledge representation schema that represents knowledge as a graph: labelled nodes in this graph correspond to domain objects/concepts but directed labelled arcs – to relations between them

glossary.definition: An object or concept within a specific domain
glossary.sentence: Labelled nodes in this graph correspond to domain objects/concepts

glossary.definition: A connection between two concepts or objects in a semantic network
glossary.sentence: Directed labelled arcs – to relations between them

glossary.definition: A relation that shows a narrowing of concepts to broader concepts
glossary.sentence: A relation "subclass-class" allows showing hierarchical relations by relating narrower concepts to broader concepts

glossary.definition: A relation represented by the title of "is a" that shows a narrower concept belongs to a broader concept
glossary.sentence: A relation "subclass-class" (also "subset-set", "subtype-type", "subkind -kind") is represented by a title of "is a"

glossary.definition: A set of concepts/objects that share common properties
glossary.sentence: A class/set/type/kind includes a set of concepts/objects that share common properties

glossary.definition: A relation represented by the title of "is an instance/example of" that links a specific object to a class/set/type/kind
glossary.sentence: A relation "instance-class/set/type/kind" is represented by the title of "is an instance/example of"

glossary.definition: A relation represented by the title of "Part of" that relates parts of an object to the whole object
glossary.sentence: A relation "part-whole" is represented by the title of "Part of"

glossary.definition: A relation represented by the title of "property/has a property of" that links objects to their properties
glossary.sentence: A relation "object-property" is represented by the title of "property/has a property of"

glossary.definition: A characteristic of an object/concept that can be measured by quantitative (numerical) or qualitative (descriptive) values
glossary.sentence: A property is a characteristic of an object/concept that can be measured by quantitative (numerical) or qualitative (descriptive) values

glossary.definition: A relation represented by the title of "value" that links properties of objects to their values
glossary.sentence: A relation "property-value" is represented by the title of "value"

glossary.definition: Relations that specify other kinds of relations (for example, like, own, travel, etc.) that cannot be represented by previously mentioned types of relations
glossary.sentence: Linguistic relations (Fig.11). They allow specifying any other kind of relations (for example, like, own, travel, etc.) that cannot be represented by the previously mentioned types of relations.

glossary.definition: an essential feature of frame-based expert systems that allows sub-class and instance frames to inherit slots and their values from class frames, and also allows overwriting of inherited values
glossary.sentence: Inheritance is an essential feature of frame-based expert systems. The generalization relation ensures inheritance in frames. Therefore, sub-class and instance frames have a possibility to inherit slots and their values from class frames, but they can also contain properties that are not represented in the class frame. Moreover, frames support overwriting of the inherited values.

glossary.definition: a remembered framework to be adapted to fit reality by changing details as necessary
glossary.sentence: When one encounters a new situation (or makes a substantial change in one's view of the present problem) one selects from memory a structure called a Frame. This is a remembered framework to be adapted to fit reality by changing details as necessary.

glossary.definition: Marvin Minsky developed frames in 1975
glossary.sentence: Marvin Minsky developed frames in 1975

glossary.definition: a system that uses frames to capture and represent knowledge
glossary.sentence: Frames are used to capture and represent knowledge in a frame-based expert system

glossary.definition: collections of related frames linked together
glossary.sentence: collections of related frames are linked together into frame-systems

glossary.definition: properties of objects/concepts
glossary.sentence: Slots are properties of objects/concepts

glossary.definition: a value that can be represented as a symbolic, numerical or boolean value, as well as it can be empty or can contain a title of another frame
glossary.sentence: Each property has a value that can be represented as a symbolic, numerical or boolean value, as well as it can be empty or can contain a title of another frame.

glossary.definition: an extension to the slot-value structure of frames as they represent extended knowledge about slot values and tell the inference engine how to process the property of the object, allow to set a slot value and control user's access to the slot value
glossary.sentence: Facets are an extension to the slot-value structure of frames as they represent extended knowledge about slot values and tell the inference engine how to process the property of the object, allow to set a slot value and control user's access to the slot value

glossary.definition: a type of value that can be specified in facets
glossary.sentence: Facets can contain value type, value constraints (maximum value, minimum value, range of values, etc.), and actions that can be performed with slot values.

glossary.definition: maximum value, minimum value, range of values, etc.
glossary.sentence: Facets can contain value type, value constraints (maximum value, minimum value, range of values, etc.), and actions that can be performed with slot values.

glossary.definition: actions that can be performed with slot values
glossary.sentence: Facets can contain value type, value constraints (maximum value, minimum value, range of values, etc.), and actions that can be performed with slot values.

glossary.definition: a frame that contains common properties of a set/class/kind/type of objects/concepts
glossary.sentence: A class/generic frame. It contains common properties of a set/class/kind/type of objects/concepts.

glossary.definition: a frame that represents a detailed knowledge about a particular object in the problem domain
glossary.sentence: An instance frame. It represents a detailed knowledge about a particular object in the problem domain

glossary.definition: a relation that relates a sub-class frame with a class frame or an instance frame with a sub-class/class frame, allowing making hierarchies of frames where the most general frame is located at the highest level and the most specific ones – at the lower levels
glossary.sentence: Generalization relation. It relates a sub-class frame with a class frame or an instance frame with a sub-class/class frame. It allows making hierarchies of frames where the most general frame is located at the highest level and the most specific ones – at the lower levels.

glossary.definition: a relation that allows representing extended knowledge about parts of objects/concepts by associating frames corresponding to the components of an object with a frame representing the object as a whole
glossary.sentence: Aggregation relation. It allows representing extended knowledge about parts of objects/concepts (Figure 9). In other words, frames corresponding to the components of an object are associated with a frame representing the object as a whole.

glossary.definition: a relation that allows defining any other type of relations by using the referencing principle
glossary.sentence: Association or semantic relation. It allows defining any other type of relations by using the referencing principle (Figure 10).

glossary.definition: a feature that allows a frame to inherit slots and slot values from several other frames simultaneously if slot titles of parent frames are unique
glossary.sentence: Multiple inheritance when a frame inherits slots and slot values from several other frames simultaneously is allowed if slot titles of parent frames are unique.

glossary.definition: a feature that allows adding regular procedures and/or demons to a slot
glossary.sentence: A slot can have a procedural attachment. In general, it is possible to add regular procedures and/or demons.

glossary.definition: a self-contained piece of computer code written by the developer of an intelligent computer system
glossary.sentence: A regular procedure is a self-contained piece of computer code written by the developer of an intelligent computer system.

glossary.definition: an implementation of actions in a frame-based expert system
glossary.sentence: Demons implement actions in a frame-based expert system

glossary.definition: a principle used to relate two frames by using a unique title of one frame as a slot value in another frame
glossary.sentence: A referencing principle is used to relate two frames. This means that a unique title of one frame is used as a slot value in another frame.

glossary.definition: The database or working memory stores the facts acquired from the user, conclusions derived in the reasoning process, confidence measures of conclusions, information coming from external programs, dead ends in the search process, and other information relevant to the case under consideration (Luger, 2009).
glossary.sentence: The working memory ensures this by storing the facts acquired from the user, conclusions derived in the reasoning process, confidence measures of conclusions, information coming from external programs, dead ends in the search process, and other information relevant to the case under consideration (Luger, 2009).

glossary.definition: Explanation facilities are essential for any expert system because an expert system provides the user with a recommendation, plan, advice, conclusion, or any other result. However, to accept the system’s recommendation, the human expert should be aware of how a particular conclusion was reached, what was the line of reasoning and whether it is valid or not (Luger, 2009; Negnevitsky , 2011). Thus, an expert system should explain its reasoning and justify the output ( Negnevitsky , 2011).
glossary.sentence: Explanation facilities are essential for any expert system because an expert system provides the user with a recommendation, plan, advice, conclusion, or any other result. However, to accept the system’s recommendation, the human expert should be aware of how a particular conclusion was reached, what was the line of reasoning and whether it is valid or not (Luger, 2009; Negnevitsky , 2011). Thus, an expert system should explain its reasoning and justify the output ( Negnevitsky , 2011).

glossary.definition: The user interacts with the system through a user interface designed as a question-and-answer, menu-driven, natural language, or graphical interface (Luger, 2009).
glossary.sentence: The user interacts with the system through a user interface designed as a question-and-answer, menu-driven, natural language, or graphical interface (Luger, 2009).

glossary.definition: The case-specific data are separated from the knowledge base (Luger, 2009). These data are assumed to be correct when they are used (Hopgood, 2001).
glossary.sentence: The case-specific data are separated from the knowledge base (Luger, 2009). These data are assumed to be correct when they are used (Hopgood, 2001).

glossary.definition: The working memory contains a description of the current state of the problem-solving process (Callan, 2003; Luger, 2009) that is called a pattern .
glossary.sentence: The working memory contains a description of the current state of the problem-solving process (Callan, 2003; Luger, 2009) that is called a pattern .

glossary.definition: The control cycle "Recognize-act" makes the basis of the inference mechanism. It implements a pattern-directed search in the knowledge base by searching for a match between the content of the working memory and production rules. While implementing the pattern-directed search, the control cycle performs the following steps: the working memory contains a pattern corresponding to the current state of the problem-solving process; the pattern is matched against the IF or THEN part (it depends on the search direction) of all rules available in the knowledge base; if several rules match the pattern, a conflict set is acquired. Production rules in the conflict set are called to be enabled. The selection of one rule from the conflict set is called conflict resolution . For this purpose, a conflict resolution strategy is used; one of the enabled rules is fired, and it changes the content of the working memory because its IF or THEN part is added to the working memory (depending on the search direction ); the control cycle repeats with the modified working memory; searching terminates when there are no more rules that match the content of the working memory. (the text fragment is based on the information given in (Luger, 2009))
glossary.sentence: The control cycle "Recognize-act" makes the basis of the inference mechanism. It implements a pattern-directed search in the knowledge base by searching for a match between the content of the working memory and production rules. While implementing the pattern-directed search, the control cycle performs the following steps: the working memory contains a pattern corresponding to the current state of the problem-solving process; the pattern is matched against the IF or THEN part (it depends on the search direction) of all rules available in the knowledge base; if several rules match the pattern, a conflict set is acquired. Production rules in the conflict set are called to be enabled. The selection of one rule from the conflict set is called conflict resolution . For this purpose, a conflict resolution strategy is used; one of the enabled rules is fired, and it changes the content of the working memory because its IF or THEN part is added to the working memory (depending on the search direction ); the control cycle repeats with the modified working memory; searching terminates when there are no more rules that match the content of the working memory. (the text fragment is based on the information given in (Luger, 2009))

glossary.definition: the set of enabled rules that are able to fire at a given time, from which one rule must be selected for execution
glossary.sentence: Conflict resolution is the method of choosing one rule to fire from those that are able to fire, i.e., from the set of enabled rules, known as the conflict set

glossary.definition: The selection of one rule from the conflict set is called conflict resolution . For this purpose, a conflict resolution strategy is used;
glossary.sentence: The selection of one rule from the conflict set is called conflict resolution . For this purpose, a conflict resolution strategy is used;

glossary.definition: a method of choosing one rule to fire from those that are able to fire, i.e., from the set of enabled rules, known as the conflict set
glossary.sentence: Conflict resolution is the method of choosing one rule to fire from those that are able to fire, i.e., from the set of enabled rules, known as the conflict set

glossary.definition: rules used by human experts that specify a condition and a conclusion, and whose structure and order are critical in controlling search and making the system behave logically
glossary.sentence: The structure of rules used by the human experts must be preserved in building a knowledge base.

glossary.definition: Forward chaining is a technique for gathering information and then inferring from it whatever can be inferred ( Negnevitsky , 2011). It is typically used for problems of interpretation because, in this type of problems, most of the data are initially given, and the system searches for their interpretation (Luger, 2009; Hopgood, 2001). However, the intelligent computer system may execute many rules in forward chaining that have nothing to do with the established goal ( Negnevitsky , 2011).
glossary.sentence: Forward chaining is a technique for gathering information and then inferring from it whatever can be inferred ( Negnevitsky , 2011). It is typically used for problems of interpretation because, in this type of problems, most of the data are initially given, and the system searches for their interpretation (Luger, 2009; Hopgood, 2001). However, the intelligent computer system may execute many rules in forward chaining that have nothing to do with the established goal ( Negnevitsky , 2011).

glossary.definition: In backward chaining , an expert system has the goal (a hypothetical solution), and the inference engine attempts to find the evidence to prove it ( Negnevitsky , 2011). Backward chaining corresponds to a decomposition of the problem’s goal into simpler subgoals; thus, backward search in an expert system corresponds roughly to the process of hypothesis testing in human problem solving (Luger, 2009; Hopgood, 2001).
glossary.sentence: In backward chaining , an expert system has the goal (a hypothetical solution), and the inference engine attempts to find the evidence to prove it ( Negnevitsky , 2011). Backward chaining corresponds to a decomposition of the problem’s goal into simpler subgoals; thus, backward search in an expert system corresponds roughly to the process of hypothesis testing in human problem solving (Luger, 2009; Hopgood, 2001).

glossary.definition: Searching terminates when there are no more rules that match the content of the working memory. (the text fragment is based on the information given in (Luger, 2009)))
glossary.sentence: Searching terminates when there are no more rules that match the content of the working memory. (the text fragment is based on the information given in (Luger, 2009)))

glossary.definition: an inference engine that selects a search direction based on the need to gather information first and then infer from it whatever can be inferred, as observed in how real domain experts solve problems
glossary.sentence: If a domain expert first needs to gather some information and then tries to infer from it whatever can be inferred, choose the forward chaining inference engine.

glossary.definition: an inference engine that selects a search direction based on starting with a hypothetical solution and then attempting to find facts to prove it, as observed in how real domain experts solve problems
glossary.sentence: However, if your domain expert begins with a hypothetical solution and then attempts to find facts to prove it, choose the backward chaining inference engine.

glossary.definition: human experts who use conditions specified in rules in a certain order and also apply rules in a certain order, such that their approach determines the conditions for successful problem solving
glossary.sentence: It is well known that problem domain experts use conditions specified in rules in a certain order and also apply rules in a certain order.

glossary.definition: the form of production rules that suggests a specific sequence of things to try, such as in the example 'if the engine does not turn over and the lights don’t come on, then check the battery'
glossary.sentence: When a mechanic says, 'if the engine does not turn over and the lights don’t come on, then check the battery', he/she suggests a specific sequence of things to try.

glossary.definition: the order in which production rules are applied, which determines how to search the knowledge base and how quickly it will be searched
glossary.sentence: The structure and the order of production rules in the knowledge base determine how to search the knowledge base and how quickly it will be searched.

glossary.definition: a conflict resolution strategy in which the conflict set is not assembled at all, and the order in which rules are selected for examination determines conflict resolution
glossary.sentence: to fire the first/the last rule found. In this scheme, the conflict set is not assembled at all, and the order in which rules are selected for examination determines conflict resolution

glossary.definition: a conflict resolution strategy in which rules are assigned an explicitly stated priority value, and the rule with the highest priority is chosen when more than one rule can fire
glossary.sentence: to fire rules based on their priorities. Rules can be written so that each has an explicitly stated priority value, and, thus, if more than one rule can fire, the one with the highest priority is chosen

glossary.definition: a conflict resolution method also known as firing more specific rules, based on the assumption that a specific rule processes more information than a general one
glossary.sentence: to fire more specific rules. This method is also known as the longest matching strategy: it is based on the assumption that a specific rule processes more information than a general one

glossary.definition: a rule that has more conditions than another rule, such as IF a AND b THEN c being more specific than IF a THEN c
glossary.sentence: A rule is more specific than another if it has more conditions: IF a THEN c IF a AND b THEN c more specific rule fire the rule that uses the data most recently entered in the working memory

glossary.definition: a conflict resolution method that fires the rule whose antecedent uses the data most recently added to the working memory, relying on time tags attached to each fact in the working memory
glossary.sentence: fire the rule that uses the data most recently entered in the working memory. This method relies on time tags attached to each fact in the working memory; thus, in the conflict set, the expert system first fires the rule whose antecedent uses the data most recently added to the working memory

glossary.definition: a characteristic of production rules that offers a unified way of storing knowledge because each rule is an independent unit of knowledge, enabling self-documentation through syntax
glossary.sentence: Production rules offer a unified way of storing knowledge because each rule is an independent unit of knowledge; thus, the very syntax of production rules enables them to be self-documented

glossary.definition: a design principle in which knowledge is stored in the knowledge base and its processing mechanism is the inference engine, allowing ease of modifying the knowledge base without changing control code and vice versa
glossary.sentence: Knowledge is stored in the knowledge base, but its processing mechanism is the inference engine. The advantages of this separation include ease of modifying the knowledge base without requiring a change in the code for program control and, conversely, the ability to alter the code for program control without changing the set of production rules

glossary.definition: a property of production rules where rules have no syntactic interactions, may only affect other rules by changing pattern in working memory, and cannot directly call another rule or set variables in other rules
glossary.sentence: Rules have no syntactic interactions. Rules may only affect the firing of other rules by changing the pattern in working memory; they may not 'call' another rule directly as if it were a subroutine, nor set the value of variables in other production rules.

glossary.definition: the ability to trace the execution of production rules at each stage of the control cycle 'Recognize-act', and to provide a meaningful explanation of the system’s current state and action through rule content
glossary.sentence: The modularity of rules and the iterative nature of their execution make it easier to trace the execution of production rules. At each stage of the control cycle 'Recognize-act', the selected rule can be displayed. In addition, because each rule corresponds to a single 'chunk' of problem-solving knowledge, the rule content can provide a meaningful explanation of the system’s current state and action.

glossary.definition: knowledge that is not fully available or not certain, which rule-based expert systems can represent and reason with by adding confidence factors
glossary.sentence: Most rule-based expert systems are capable of representing and reasoning with incomplete and uncertain knowledge by adding confidence factors.

glossary.definition: the issues that include difficulty in determining the contribution of each specific rule in a large number of rules, the computational resource consumption due to searching the whole knowledge base in each iteration, and inability to learn from experience or understand which rules are out-of-date
glossary.sentence: However, rule-based expert systems have also disadvantages: in the case of the considerable number of production rules, it is difficult to find out the contribution of each specific rule to the problem-solving process; in each iteration, the inference mechanism searches the whole knowledge base. It could be a very computational resource-consuming process. Expert systems with a large set of rules (over 100 rules) can be slow, and thus can be unsuitable for real-time applications; rule-based expert systems are not able to learn from the experience and understand which rules are already out-of-date.

glossary.definition: the memory component in which facts are stored and from which rules extract data to evaluate their conditions, with time tags attached to facts to enable time-based rule firing
glossary.sentence: fire the rule that uses the data most recently entered in the working memory. This method relies on time tags attached to each fact in the working memory
